package org.apache.kafka.clients.consumer;

import org.apache.kafka.common.TopicPartition;

import java.time.Duration;
import java.util.Collection;
import java.util.Map;

/**
 * A callback interface that the user can implement to trigger custom actions when a commit request completes. The callback
 * may be executed in any thread calling {@link Consumer#poll(java.time.Duration) poll()}.
 */
@FunctionalInterface
public interface OffsetCommitCallback {

    /**
     * A callback method the user can implement to provide asynchronous handling of commit request completion.
     * This method will be called when the commit request sent to the server has been acknowledged.
     *
     * @param offsets A map of the offsets and associated metadata that this callback applies to
     * @param exception The exception thrown during processing of the request, or null if the commit completed successfully
     *
     * @throws org.apache.kafka.clients.consumer.CommitFailedException if the commit failed and cannot be retried.
     *             This can only occur if you are using automatic group management with {@link KafkaConsumer#subscribe(Collection)},
     *             or if there is an active group with the same groupId which is using group management.
     * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.RebalanceInProgressException if the commit failed because
     *            it is in the middle of a rebalance. In such cases
     *            commit could be retried after the rebalance is completed with the {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(Duration)} call.
     * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException if {@link KafkaConsumer#wakeup()} is called before or while this
     *             function is called
     * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException if the calling thread is interrupted before or while
     *             this function is called
     * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.AuthorizationException if not authorized to the topic or to the
     *             configured groupId. See the exception for more details
     * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.KafkaException for any other unrecoverable errors (e.g. if offset metadata
     *             is too large or if the committed offset is invalid).
     */
    void onComplete(Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndMetadata> offsets, Exception exception);
}

/**
 * A callback interface that the user can implement to trigger custom actions when a commit request completes. The callback
 * may be executed in any thread calling {@link Consumer#poll(java.time.Duration) poll()}.
 */
@FunctionalInterface
public interface OffsetCommitCallback {

    /**
     * A callback method the user can implement to provide asynchronous handling of commit request completion.
     * This method will be called when the commit request sent to the server has been acknowledged.
     *
     * @param offsets A map of the offsets and associated metadata that this callback applies to
     * @param exception The exception thrown during processing of the request, or null if the commit completed successfully
     *

     *             This can only occur if you are using automatic group management with {@link KafkaConsumer#subscribe(Collection)},
     *             or if there is an active group with the same groupId which is using group management.
     * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.RebalanceInProgressException if the commit failed because
     *            it is in the middle of a rebalance. In such cases
     *            commit could be retried after the rebalance is completed with the {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(Duration)} call.
     * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException if {@link KafkaConsumer#wakeup()} is called before or while this
     *             function is called
     * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException if the calling thread is interrupted before or while
     *             this function is called
     * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.AuthorizationException if not authorized to the topic or to the
     *             configured groupId. See the exception for more details
     * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.KafkaException for any other unrecoverable errors (e.g. if offset metadata
     *             is too large or if the committed offset is invalid).
     */
    void onComplete(Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndMetadata> offsets, Exception exception);
}

    22→import java.util.Collection;
    23→import java.util.Map;

    25→/**
    26→ * A callback interface that the user can implement to trigger custom actions when a commit request completes. The callback
    27→ * may be executed in any thread calling {@link Consumer#poll(java.time.Duration) poll()}.
    28→ */
    29→@FunctionalInterface

    31→
    32→    /**
    33→     * A callback method the user can implement to provide asynchronous handling of commit request completion.
    34→     * This method will be called when the commit request sent to the server has been acknowledged.
    35→     *
    36→     * @param offsets A map of the offsets and associated metadata that this callback applies to
    37→     * @param exception The exception thrown during processing of the request, or null if the commit completed successfully
    38→     *
    39→     * @throws org.apache.kafka.clients.consumer.CommitFailedException if the commit failed and cannot be retried.
    40→     *             This can only occur if you are using automatic group management with {@link KafkaConsumer#subscribe(Collection)},
    41→     *             or if there is an active group with the same groupId which is using group management.
    42→     * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.RebalanceInProgressException if the commit failed because
    43→     *            it is in the middle of a rebalance. In such cases
    44→     *            commit could be retried after the rebalance is completed with the {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(Duration)} call.
    45→     * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException if {@link KafkaConsumer#wakeup()} is called before or while this
    46→     *             function is called
    47→     * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException if the calling thread is interrupted before or while
    48→     *             this function is called
    49→     * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.AuthorizationException if not authorized to the topic or to the
    50→     *             configured groupId. See the exception for more details
    51→     * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.KafkaException for any other unrecoverable errors (e.g. if offset metadata
    52→     *             is too large or if the committed offset is invalid).
    53→     */
    54→    void onComplete(Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndMetadata> offsets, Exception exception);
    55→}

500→ * We have intentionally avoided implementing a particular threading model for processing. This leaves several 501→ * options for implementing multi-threaded processing of records. 502→ * 503→ *

1. One Consumer Per Thread

504→ * 505→ * A simple option is to give each thread its own consumer instance. Here are the pros and cons of this approach: 506→ *
    507→ *
  • PRO: It is the easiest to implement 508→ *
  • PRO: It is often the fastest as no inter-thread co-ordination is needed 509→ *
  • PRO: It makes in-order processing on a per-partition basis very easy to implement (each thread just 510→ * processes messages in the order it receives them). 511→ *
  • CON: More consumers means more TCP connections to the cluster (one per thread). In general Kafka handles 512→ * connections very efficiently so this is generally a small cost. 513→ *
  • CON: Multiple consumers means more requests being sent to the server and slightly less batching of data 514→ * which can cause some drop in I/O throughput. 515→ *
  • CON: The number of total threads across all processes will be limited by the total number of partitions. 516→ *
517→ * 518→ *

2. Decouple Consumption and Processing

519→ * 520→ * Another alternative is to have one or more consumer threads that do all data consumption and hands off 521→ * {@link ConsumerRecords} instances to a blocking queue consumed by a pool of processor threads that actually handle 522→ * the record processing. 523→ * 524→ * This option likewise has pros and cons: 525→ *
    526→ *
  • PRO: This option allows independently scaling the number of consumers and processors. This makes it 527→ * possible to have a single consumer that feeds many processor threads, avoiding any limitation on partitions. 528→ *
  • CON: Guaranteeing order across the processors requires particular care as the threads will execute 529→ * independently an earlier chunk of data may actually be processed after a later chunk of data just due to the luck of 530→ * thread execution timing. For processing that has no ordering requirements this is not a problem. 531→ *
  • CON: Manually committing the position becomes harder as it requires that all threads co-ordinate to ensure 532→ * that processing is complete for that partition. 533→ *
534→ * 535→ * There are many possible variations on this approach. For example each processor thread can have its own queue, and 536→ * the consumer threads can hash into these queues using the TopicPartition to ensure in-order consumption and simplify 537→ * commit. 538→ */ 539→public class KafkaConsumer implements Consumer { 540→ 541→ private static final ConsumerDelegateCreator CREATOR = new ConsumerDelegateCreator(); 542→ 543→ private final ConsumerDelegate delegate; 544→ 545→ /** 546→ * A consumer is instantiated by providing a set of key-value pairs as configuration. * We have intentionally avoided implementing a particular threading model for processing. This leaves several * options for implementing multi-threaded processing of records. * *

1. One Consumer Per Thread

* * A simple option is to give each thread its own consumer instance. Here are the pros and cons of this approach: *
    *
  • PRO: It is the easiest to implement *
  • PRO: It is often the fastest as no inter-thread co-ordination is needed *
  • PRO: It makes in-order processing on a per-partition basis very easy to implement (each thread just * processes messages in the order it receives them). *
  • CON: More consumers means more TCP connections to the cluster (one per thread). In general Kafka handles * connections very efficiently so this is generally a small cost. *
  • CON: Multiple consumers means more requests being sent to the server and slightly less batching of data * which can cause some drop in I/O throughput. *
  • CON: The number of total threads across all processes will be limited by the total number of partitions. *
* *

2. Decouple Consumption and Processing

* * Another alternative is to have one or more consumer threads that do all data consumption and hands off * {@link ConsumerRecords} instances to a blocking queue consumed by a pool of processor threads that actually handle * the record processing. * * This option likewise has pros and cons: *
    *
  • PRO: This option allows independently scaling the number of consumers and processors. This makes it * possible to have a single consumer that feeds many processor threads, avoiding any limitation on partitions. *
  • CON: Guaranteeing order across the processors requires particular care as the threads will execute * independently an earlier chunk of data may actually be processed after a later chunk of data just due to the luck of * thread execution timing. For processing that has no ordering requirements this is not a problem. *
  • CON: Manually committing the position becomes harder as it requires that all threads co-ordinate to ensure * that processing is complete for that partition. *
* * There are many possible variations on this approach. For example each processor thread can have its own queue, and * the consumer threads can hash into these queues using the TopicPartition to ensure in-order consumption and simplify * commit. */ public class KafkaConsumer implements Consumer { private static final ConsumerDelegateCreator CREATOR = new ConsumerDelegateCreator(); private final ConsumerDelegate delegate; /** * A consumer is instantiated by providing a set of key-value pairs as configuration. Valid configuration strings * are documented here. Values can be * either strings or objects of the appropriate type (for example a numeric configuration would accept either the * string "42" or the integer 42). *

* Valid configuration strings are documented at {@link ConsumerConfig}. *

* Note: after creating a {@code KafkaConsumer} you must always {@link #close()} it to avoid resource leaks. * * @param configs The consumer configs */ public KafkaConsumer(Map configs) { this(configs, null, null); } /** * A consumer is instantiated by providing a {@link java.util.Properties} object as configuration. *

* Valid configuration strings are documented at {@link ConsumerConfig}. *

* Note: after creating a {@code KafkaConsumer} you must always {@link #close()} it to avoid resource leaks. * * @param properties The consumer configuration properties */ public KafkaConsumer(Properties properties) { this(properties, null, null); } /** * A consumer is instantiated by providing a {@link java.util.Properties} object as configuration, and a * key and a value {@link Deserializer}. *

* Valid configuration strings are documented at {@link ConsumerConfig}. *

* Note: after creating a {@code KafkaConsumer} you must always {@link #close()} it to avoid resource leaks. * * @param properties The consumer configuration properties * @param keyDeserializer The deserializer for key that implements {@link Deserializer}. The configure() method * won't be called in the consumer when the deserializer is passed in directly. * @param valueDeserializer The deserializer for value that implements {@link Deserializer}. The configure() method * won't be called in the consumer when the deserializer is passed in directly. */ public KafkaConsumer(Properties properties, Deserializer keyDeserializer, Deserializer valueDeserializer) { this(propsToMap(properties), keyDeserializer, valueDeserializer); } /** * A consumer is instantiated by providing a set of key-value pairs as configuration, and a key and a value {@link Deserializer}. *

* Valid configuration strings are documented at {@link ConsumerConfig}. *

* Note: after creating a {@code KafkaConsumer} you must always {@link #close()} it to avoid resource leaks. * * @param configs The consumer configs * @param keyDeserializer The deserializer for key that implements {@link Deserializer}. The configure() method * won't be called in the consumer when the deserializer is passed in directly. * @param valueDeserializer The deserializer for value that implements {@link Deserializer}. The configure() method * won't be called in the consumer when the deserializer is passed in directly. */ public KafkaConsumer(Map configs, Deserializer keyDeserializer, Deserializer valueDeserializer) { this(new ConsumerConfig(ConsumerConfig.appendDeserializerToConfig(configs, keyDeserializer, valueDeserializer)), keyDeserializer, valueDeserializer); } KafkaConsumer(ConsumerConfig config, Deserializer keyDeserializer, Deserializer valueDeserializer) { delegate = CREATOR.create(config, keyDeserializer, valueDeserializer); } KafkaConsumer(LogContext logContext, Time time, ConsumerConfig config, Deserializer keyDeserializer, Deserializer valueDeserializer, KafkaClient client, SubscriptionState subscriptions, ConsumerMetadata metadata, List assignors) { delegate = CREATOR.create( logContext, time, config, keyDeserializer, valueDeserializer, client, subscriptions, metadata, assignors ); } /** * Get the set of partitions currently assigned to this consumer. If subscription happened by directly assigning * partitions using {@link #assign(Collection)} then this will simply return the same partitions that * were assigned. If topic subscription was used, then this will give the set of topic partitions currently assigned * to the consumer (which may be none if the assignment hasn't happened yet, or the partitions are in the * process of getting reassigned). * @return The set of partitions currently assigned to this consumer */ public Set assignment() { return delegate.assignment(); } /** * Get the current subscription. Will return the same topics used in the most recent call to * {@link #subscribe(Collection, ConsumerRebalanceListener)}, or an empty set if no such call has been made. * @return The set of topics currently subscribed to */ public Set subscription() { return delegate.subscription(); } /** * Subscribe to the given list of topics to get dynamically * assigned partitions. Topic subscriptions are not incremental. This list will replace the current * assignment (if there is one). Note that it is not possible to combine topic subscription with group management * with manual partition assignment through {@link #assign(Collection)}. * * If the given list of topics is empty, it is treated the same as {@link #unsubscribe()}. * *

* As part of group management, the group coordinator will keep track of the list of consumers that belong to a particular * group and will trigger a rebalance operation if any one of the following events are triggered: *

    *
  • Number of partitions change for any of the subscribed topics *
  • A subscribed topic is created or deleted *
  • An existing member of the consumer group is shutdown or fails *
  • A new member is added to the consumer group *
*

* When any of these events are triggered, the provided listener will be invoked in this way: *

    *
  • {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener#onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} will be invoked with the partitions to revoke, before re-assigning those partitions to another consumer.
    • *
  • {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener#onPartitionsAssigned(Collection)} will be invoked when the rebalance completes (even if no new partitions are assigned to the consumer)
    • *
* Note that rebalances will only occur during an active call to {@link #poll(Duration)}, so callbacks will * also only be invoked during that time. * * The provided listener will immediately override any listener set in a previous call to subscribe. * It is guaranteed, however, that the partitions revoked/assigned through this interface are from topics * subscribed in this call. See {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener} for more details. * * @param topics The list of topics to subscribe to * @param listener Non-null listener instance to get notifications on partition assignment/revocation for the * subscribed topics * @throws IllegalArgumentException If topics is null or contains null or empty elements, or if listener is null * @throws IllegalStateException If {@code subscribe()} is called previously with pattern, or assign is called * previously (without a subsequent call to {@link #unsubscribe()}), or if not * configured at-least one partition assignment strategy */ @Override

  • CON: More consumers means more TCP connections to the cluster (one per thread). In general Kafka handles 512→ * connections very efficiently so this is generally a small cost. 513→ *
  • CON: Multiple consumers means more requests being sent to the server and slightly less batching of data 514→ * which can cause some drop in I/O throughput. 515→ *

  • PRO: This option allows independently scaling the number of consumers and processors. This makes it 527→ * possible to have a single consumer that feeds many processor threads, avoiding any limitation on partitions. 528→ *
  • CON: Guaranteeing order across the processors requires particular care as the threads will execute 529→ * independently an earlier chunk of data may actually be processed after a later chunk of data just due to the luck of 530→ * thread execution timing.   • CON: Manually committing the position becomes harder as it requires that all threads co-ordinate to ensure 532→ * that processing is complete for that partition. 533→ *

The configure() method 586→ * won't be called in the consumer when the deserializer is passed in directly. 587→ */ 588→ public KafkaConsumer(Properties properties, 589→ Deserializer keyDeserializer, 590→ Deserializer valueDeserializer) { 591→ this(propsToMap(properties), keyDeserializer, valueDeserializer); 592→ } 593→ 594→ /** 595→ * A consumer is instantiated by providing a set of key-value pairs as configuration, and a key and a value {@link Deserializer}. 596→ *

The configure() method 605→ * won't be called in the consumer when the deserializer is passed in directly. 606→ */ 607→ public KafkaConsumer(Map configs, 608→ Deserializer keyDeserializer, 609→ Deserializer valueDeserializer) { 610→ this(new ConsumerConfig(ConsumerConfig.appendDeserializerToConfig(configs, keyDeserializer, valueDeserializer)), 611→ keyDeserializer, valueDeserializer); 612→ } 613→ 614→ KafkaConsumer(ConsumerConfig config, Deserializer keyDeserializer, Deserializer valueDeserializer) { 615→ delegate = CREATOR.create(config, keyDeserializer, valueDeserializer); 616→ } 617→ 618→ KafkaConsumer(LogContext logContext, 619→ Time time, 620→ ConsumerConfig config, 621→ Deserializer keyDeserializer, 622→ Deserializer valueDeserializer, 623→ KafkaClient client, 624→ SubscriptionState subscriptions, 625→ ConsumerMetadata metadata, 626→ List assignors) { 627→ delegate = CREATOR.create( 628→ logContext, 629→ time, 630→ config, 631→ keyDeserializer, 632→ valueDeserializer, 633→ client, 634→ subscriptions, 635→ metadata, 636→ assignors 637→ ); 638→ } 639→ 640→ /** 641→ * Get the set of partitions currently assigned to this consumer. Will return the same topics used in the most recent call to 654→ * {@link #subscribe(Collection, ConsumerRebalanceListener)}, or an empty set if no such call has been made. 655→ * @return The set of topics currently subscribed to 656→ */ 657→ public Set subscription() { 658→ return delegate.subscription(); 659→ } 660→ 661→ /** 662→ * Subscribe to the given list of topics to get dynamically 663→ * assigned partitions. * We have intentionally avoided implementing a particular threading model for processing. This leaves several * options for implementing multi-threaded processing of records. * *

1. One Consumer Per Thread

* * A simple option is to give each thread its own consumer instance. Here are the pros and cons of this approach: *
    *
  • PRO: It is the easiest to implement *
  • PRO: It is often the fastest as no inter-thread co-ordination is needed *
  • PRO: It makes in-order processing on a per-partition basis very easy to implement (each thread just * processes messages in the order it receives them). *
  • CON: More consumers means more TCP connections to the cluster (one per thread). In general Kafka handles * connections very efficiently so this is generally a small cost. *
  • CON: Multiple consumers means more requests being sent to the server and slightly less batching of data * which can cause some drop in I/O throughput. *
  • CON: The number of total threads across all processes will be limited by the total number of partitions. *
* *

2. Decouple Consumption and Processing

* * Another alternative is to have one or more consumer threads that do all data consumption and hands off * {@link ConsumerRecords} instances to a blocking queue consumed by a pool of processor threads that actually handle * the record processing. * * This option likewise has pros and cons: *
    *
  • PRO: This option allows independently scaling the number of consumers and processors. This makes it * possible to have a single consumer that feeds many processor threads, avoiding any limitation on partitions. *
  • CON: Guaranteeing order across the processors requires particular care as the threads will execute * independently an earlier chunk of data may actually be processed after a later chunk of data just due to the luck of * thread execution timing. For processing that has no ordering requirements this is not a problem. *
  • CON: Manually committing the position becomes harder as it requires that all threads co-ordinate to ensure * that processing is complete for that partition. *
* * There are many possible variations on this approach. For example each processor thread can have its own queue, and * the consumer threads can hash into these queues using the TopicPartition to ensure in-order consumption and simplify * commit. */ public class KafkaConsumer implements Consumer { private static final ConsumerDelegateCreator CREATOR = new ConsumerDelegateCreator(); private final ConsumerDelegate delegate; /** * A consumer is instantiated by providing a set of key-value pairs as configuration. Valid configuration strings * are documented here. Values can be * either strings or objects of the appropriate type (for example a numeric configuration would accept either the * string "42" or the integer 42). *

* Valid configuration strings are documented at {@link ConsumerConfig}. *

* Note: after creating a {@code KafkaConsumer} you must always {@link #close()} it to avoid resource leaks. * * @param configs The consumer configs */ public KafkaConsumer(Map configs) { this(configs, null, null); } /** * A consumer is instantiated by providing a {@link java.util.Properties} object as configuration. *

* Valid configuration strings are documented at {@link ConsumerConfig}. *

* Note: after creating a {@code KafkaConsumer} you must always {@link #close()} it to avoid resource leaks. * * @param properties The consumer configuration properties */ public KafkaConsumer(Properties properties) { this(properties, null, null); } /** * A consumer is instantiated by providing a {@link java.util.Properties} object as configuration, and a * key and a value {@link Deserializer}. *

* Valid configuration strings are documented at {@link ConsumerConfig}. *

* Note: after creating a {@code KafkaConsumer} you must always {@link #close()} it to avoid resource leaks. * * @param properties The consumer configuration properties * @param keyDeserializer The deserializer for key that implements {@link Deserializer}. The configure() method * won't be called in the consumer when the deserializer is passed in directly. * @param valueDeserializer The deserializer for value that implements {@link Deserializer}. The configure() method * won't be called in the consumer when the deserializer is passed in directly. */ public KafkaConsumer(Properties properties, Deserializer keyDeserializer, Deserializer valueDeserializer) { this(propsToMap(properties), keyDeserializer, valueDeserializer); } /** * A consumer is instantiated by providing a set of key-value pairs as configuration, and a key and a value {@link Deserializer}. *

* Valid configuration strings are documented at {@link ConsumerConfig}. *

* Note: after creating a {@code KafkaConsumer} you must always {@link #close()} it to avoid resource leaks. * * @param configs The consumer configs * @param keyDeserializer The deserializer for key that implements {@link Deserializer}. The configure() method * won't be called in the consumer when the deserializer is passed in directly. * @param valueDeserializer The deserializer for value that implements {@link Deserializer}. The configure() method * won't be called in the consumer when the deserializer is passed in directly. */ public KafkaConsumer(Map configs, Deserializer keyDeserializer, Deserializer valueDeserializer) { this(new ConsumerConfig(ConsumerConfig.appendDeserializerToConfig(configs, keyDeserializer, valueDeserializer)), keyDeserializer, valueDeserializer); } KafkaConsumer(ConsumerConfig config, Deserializer keyDeserializer, Deserializer valueDeserializer) { delegate = CREATOR.create(config, keyDeserializer, valueDeserializer); } KafkaConsumer(LogContext logContext, Time time, ConsumerConfig config, Deserializer keyDeserializer, Deserializer valueDeserializer, KafkaClient client, SubscriptionState subscriptions, ConsumerMetadata metadata, List assignors) { delegate = CREATOR.create( logContext, time, config, keyDeserializer, valueDeserializer, client, subscriptions, metadata, assignors ); } /** * Get the set of partitions currently assigned to this consumer. If subscription happened by directly assigning * partitions using {@link #assign(Collection)} then this will simply return the same partitions that * were assigned. If topic subscription was used, then this will give the set of topic partitions currently assigned * to the consumer (which may be none if the assignment hasn't happened yet, or the partitions are in the * process of getting reassigned). * @return The set of partitions currently assigned to this consumer */ public Set assignment() { return delegate.assignment(); } /** * Get the current subscription. Will return the same topics used in the most recent call to * {@link #subscribe(Collection, ConsumerRebalanceListener)}, or an empty set if no such call has been made. * @return The set of topics currently subscribed to */ public Set subscription() { return delegate.subscription(); } /** * Subscribe to the given list of topics to get dynamically * assigned partitions. Topic subscriptions are not incremental. This list will replace the current * assignment (if there is one). Note that it is not possible to combine topic subscription with group management * with manual partition assignment through {@link #assign(Collection)}. * * If the given list of topics is empty, it is treated the same as {@link #unsubscribe()}. * *

* As part of group management, the group coordinator will keep track of the list of consumers that belong to a particular * group and will trigger a rebalance operation if any one of the following events are triggered: *

    *
  • Number of partitions change for any of the subscribed topics *
  • A subscribed topic is created or deleted *
  • An existing member of the consumer group is shutdown or fails *
  • A new member is added to the consumer group *
*

* When any of these events are triggered, the provided listener will be invoked in this way: *

    *
  • {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener#onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} will be invoked with the partitions to revoke, before re-assigning those partitions to another consumer.
    • *
  • {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener#onPartitionsAssigned(Collection)} will be invoked when the rebalance completes (even if no new partitions are assigned to the consumer)
    • *
* Note that rebalances will only occur during an active call to {@link #poll(Duration)}, so callbacks will * also only be invoked during that time. * * The provided listener will immediately override any listener set in a previous call to subscribe. * It is guaranteed, however, that the partitions revoked/assigned through this interface are from topics * subscribed in this call. See {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener} for more details. * * @param topics The list of topics to subscribe to * @param listener Non-null listener instance to get notifications on partition assignment/revocation for the * subscribed topics * @throws IllegalArgumentException If topics is null or contains null or empty elements, or if listener is null * @throws IllegalStateException If {@code subscribe()} is called previously with pattern, or assign is called * previously (without a subsequent call to {@link #unsubscribe()}), or if not * configured at-least one partition assignment strategy */ @Override

* On each poll, consumer will try to use the last consumed offset as the starting offset and fetch sequentially. The last * consumed offset can be manually set through {@link #seek(TopicPartition, long)} or automatically set as the last committed * offset for the subscribed list of partitions * *

* This method returns immediately if there are records available or if the position advances past control records * or aborted transactions when isolation.level=read_committed. * Otherwise, it will await the passed timeout. See the exception for more details 887→ * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.KafkaException for any other unrecoverable errors (e.g. invalid groupId or 888→ * session timeout, errors deserializing key/value pairs, your rebalance callback thrown exceptions, 889→ * or any new error cases in future versions) 890→ * @throws java.lang.IllegalArgumentException if the timeout value is negative 891→ * @throws java.lang.IllegalStateException if the consumer is not subscribed to any topics or manually assigned any 892→ * partitions to consume from 893→ * @throws java.lang.ArithmeticException if the timeout is greater than {@link Long#MAX_VALUE} milliseconds. 894→ * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InvalidTopicException if the current subscription contains any invalid 895→ * topic (per {@link org.apache.kafka.common.internals.Topic#validate(String)}) 896→ * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.UnsupportedVersionException if the consumer attempts to fetch stable offsets 897→ * when the broker doesn't support this feature. Also, if the consumer attempts to subscribe to a 898→ * SubscriptionPattern via {@link #subscribe(SubscriptionPattern)} or 899→ * {@link #subscribe(SubscriptionPattern, ConsumerRebalanceListener)} and the broker doesn't 900→ * support this feature. 901→ * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.FencedInstanceIdException if this consumer instance gets fenced by broker. 902→ */ 903→ @Override 904→ public ConsumerRecords poll(final Duration timeout) { 905→ return delegate.poll(timeout); 906→ } 907→ 908→ /** 909→ * Commit offsets returned on the last {@link #poll(Duration) poll()} for all the subscribed list of topics and 910→ * partitions. 911→ *

912→ * This commits offsets only to Kafka. The offsets committed using this API will be used on the first fetch after 913→ * every rebalance and also on startup. As such, if you need to store offsets in anything other than Kafka, this API 914→ * should not be used. 915→ *

916→ * This is a synchronous commit and will block until either the commit succeeds, an unrecoverable error is 917→ * encountered (in which case it is thrown to the caller), or the timeout specified by {@code default.api.timeout.ms} expires 918→ * (in which case a {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.TimeoutException} is thrown to the caller). 919→ *

920→ * Note that asynchronous offset commits sent previously with the {@link #commitAsync(OffsetCommitCallback)} 921→ * (or similar) are guaranteed to have their callbacks invoked prior to completion of this method, 922→ * but only when the consumer is using the consumer group protocol. 923→ * 924→ * @throws org.apache.kafka.clients.consumer.CommitFailedException if the commit failed and cannot be retried. 925→ * This fatal error can only occur if you are using automatic group management with {@link #subscribe(Collection)}, 926→ * or if there is an active group with the same group.id which is using group management. In such cases, 927→ * when you are trying to commit to partitions that are no longer assigned to this consumer because the 928→ * consumer is for example no longer part of the group this exception would be thrown. 929→ * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.RebalanceInProgressException if the consumer instance is in the middle of a rebalance 930→ * so it is not yet determined which partitions would be assigned to the consumer. In such cases you can first 931→ * complete the rebalance by calling {@link #poll(Duration)} and commit can be reconsidered afterwards. 932→ * NOTE when you reconsider committing after the rebalance, the assigned partitions may have changed, 933→ * and also for those partitions that are still assigned their fetch positions may have changed too 934→ * if more records are returned from the {@link #poll(Duration)} call. 935→ * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException if {@link #wakeup()} is called before or while this 936→ * function is called 937→ * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException if the calling thread is interrupted before or while 938→ * this function is called 939→ * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.AuthenticationException if authentication fails. See the exception for more details 940→ * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.AuthorizationException if not authorized to the topic or to the 941→ * configured groupId. See the exception for more details 942→ * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.KafkaException for any other unrecoverable errors (e.g. if offset metadata 943→ * is too large or if the topic does not exist). 944→ * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.TimeoutException if the timeout specified by {@code default.api.timeout.ms} expires 945→ * before successful completion of the offset commit 946→ * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.FencedInstanceIdException if this consumer is using the classic group protocol 947→ * and this instance gets fenced by broker. 948→ */ 949→ @Override 950→ public void commitSync() { 951→ delegate.commitSync(); 952→ } 953→ 954→ /** 955→ * Commit offsets returned on the last {@link #poll(Duration) poll()} for all the subscribed list of topics and 956→ * partitions. 957→ *

958→ * This commits offsets only to Kafka. The offsets committed using this API will be used on the first fetch after 959→ * every rebalance and also on startup. As such, if you need to store offsets in anything other than Kafka, this API 960→ * should not be used. 961→ *

962→ * This is a synchronous commit and will block until either the commit succeeds, an unrecoverable error is 963→ * encountered (in which case it is thrown to the caller), or the passed timeout expires. 964→ *

965→ * Note that asynchronous offset commits sent previously with the {@link #commitAsync(OffsetCommitCallback)} 966→ * (or similar) are guaranteed to have their callbacks invoked prior to completion of this method, 967→ * but only when the consumer is using the consumer group protocol. 968→ * 969→ * @throws org.apache.kafka.clients.consumer.CommitFailedException if the commit failed and cannot be retried. 970→ * This can only occur if you are using automatic group management with {@link #subscribe(Collection)}, 971→ * or if there is an active group with the same group.id which is using group management. In such cases, 972→ * when you are trying to commit to partitions that are no longer assigned to this consumer because the 973→ * consumer is for example no longer part of the group this exception would be thrown. 974→ * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.RebalanceInProgressException if the consumer instance is in the middle of a rebalance 975→ * so it is not yet determined which partitions would be assigned to the consumer. In such cases you can first 976→ * complete the rebalance by calling {@link #poll(Duration)} and commit can be reconsidered afterwards. 977→ * NOTE when you reconsider committing after the rebalance, the assigned partitions may have changed, 978→ * and also for those partitions that are still assigned their fetch positions may have changed too 979→ * if more records are returned from the {@link #poll(Duration)} call. steps.message: Now I'll create comprehensive API reference documentation for the Kafka KafkaConsumer. Let me write this documentation covering all the required aspects.

arguments.content:
# Apache Kafka KafkaConsumer API Reference

## Table of Contents

1. [Overview](#overview)
2. [Threading Model](#threading-model)
3. [Core Types](#core-types)
4. [Consumer Lifecycle](#consumer-lifecycle)
5. [Subscription and Assignment](#subscription-and-assignment)
6. [Polling and Data Fetching](#polling-and-data-fetching)
7. [Offset Management](#offset-management)
8. [Flow Control and Position Management](#flow-control-and-position-management)
9. [Rebalancing and Group Coordination](#rebalancing-and-group-coordination)
10. [Transactional Message Consumption](#transactional-message-consumption)
11. [Metadata and Monitoring](#metadata-and-monitoring)
12. [Error Handling](#error-handling)
13. [Configuration-Driven Behaviors](#configuration-driven-behaviors)
14. [Common Patterns and Best Practices](#common-patterns-and-best-practices)

---

## Overview

`KafkaConsumer` is a client that consumes records from an Apache Kafka cluster. It transparently handles broker failures, adapts to partition migration, and interacts with brokers to support consumer group-based load balancing.

### Key Characteristics

- **Not Thread-Safe**: The consumer is not thread-safe and access must be synchronized except for `wakeup()`
- **Single-Threaded Event Loop**: Internally uses an event loop to maintain a single logical consumer thread
- **Partitioned Topic Support**: Handles both dynamic subscription and manual partition assignment
- **Group Coordination**: Integrates with the consumer group protocol for distributed load balancing
- **Flexible Offset Management**: Supports automatic, manual, and external offset storage strategies

### Resource Management

After creating a `KafkaConsumer`, you must always call `close()` to avoid resource leaks, as the consumer maintains TCP connections to brokers and background threads for heartbeats and fetch operations.

---

## Threading Model

### Single-Threaded Design

The Kafka consumer is **not thread-safe**. Only one thread should call consumer methods at a time. Concurrent calls from multiple threads will result in `ConcurrentModificationException`.

**Exception**: The `wakeup()` method is thread-safe and can be called from external threads to interrupt blocking operations.

### Wakeup Mechanism

```java
// Thread-safe interruption pattern
public void wakeup() {
    // Can be called from any thread
    // Causes WakeupException in the blocked poll() call
}
```

### Thread Interruption

Thread interrupts are also supported (will throw `InterruptException`) but are discouraged because they may abort clean consumer shutdown. Use `wakeup()` when possible.

### Multi-Threaded Processing Patterns

#### Pattern 1: One Consumer Per Thread
- Simplest to implement
- One consumer instance per thread with dedicated partitions
- Trade-off: More TCP connections and server requests

#### Pattern 2: Decoupled Consumption and Processing
- Single consumer thread feeds records to a blocking queue
- Multiple processor threads consume from the queue
- Enables independent scaling of consumers and processors
- Challenge: Ensuring ordering and coordinating commits across threads

---

## Core Types

### KafkaConsumer<K, V>

The main consumer class for fetching records from Kafka topics.

**Type Parameters:**
- `K`: Type of the record key
- `V`: Type of the record value

### ConsumerRecord<K, V>

Represents a single message fetched from Kafka.

**Key Fields:**
- `String topic`: Topic from which the record was fetched
- `int partition`: Partition number within the topic
- `long offset`: The offset of this record within its partition
- `long timestamp`: Timestamp of the record (type depends on `timestamp.type` config)
- `K key`: The record's key (null if no key)
- `V value`: The record's payload
- `Headers headers`: Optional headers attached to the record
- `Optional<Integer> leaderEpoch`: Leader epoch for this record

**Thread Safety Note:** `ConsumerRecord` instances are not thread-safe. Concurrent access by multiple threads may result in undefined behavior. Each individual `Header` instance is read thread-safe.

### ConsumerRecords<K, V>

A collection of consumer records returned by a single `poll()` call.

**Key Methods:**
- `int count()`: Total number of records
- `boolean isEmpty()`: Whether collection is empty
- `Iterable<ConsumerRecord<K, V>> records(TopicPartition partition)`: Records from a specific partition
- `Set<TopicPartition> partitions()`: All partitions with records
- `Map<TopicPartition, Long> nextOffsets()`: Next offset to fetch for each partition

### ConsumerRebalanceListener

Callback interface for partition assignment changes.

**Methods:**

```java
public interface ConsumerRebalanceListener {
    /**
     * Invoked when partitions are revoked during rebalance.
     * Called before new assignment, allowing cleanup and offset commits.
     * Under eager rebalancing: called with all assigned partitions
     * Under cooperative rebalancing: called only with revoked partitions (if non-empty)
     */
    void onPartitionsRevoked(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions);

    /**
     * Invoked after rebalance completes and new partitions are assigned.
     * Called even if no new partitions were added (to signal rebalance completion).
     * Use this to initialize positions for newly assigned partitions.
     */
    void onPartitionsAssigned(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions);

    /**
     * Invoked when partitions are lost (not revoked gracefully).
     * Occurs when session timeout expires or fatal errors occur.
     * Do not attempt offset commits in this callback.
     * Default implementation delegates to onPartitionsRevoked().
     */
    default void onPartitionsLost(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions) {
        onPartitionsRevoked(partitions);
    }
}
```

**Critical Behavioral Notes:**
- Callbacks execute in the **user thread** as part of `poll()` calls
- `onPartitionsRevoked()` always executes before `onPartitionsAssigned()` under normal conditions
- Exceptions thrown from callbacks propagate to the `poll()` call
- `WakeupException` and `InterruptException` from nested consumer calls propagate through the callback
- Rebalances only occur during `poll()` calls, so callbacks only execute during polling

### OffsetAndMetadata

Encapsulates an offset and optional metadata string.

**Constructor:**
```java
public OffsetAndMetadata(long offset)
public OffsetAndMetadata(long offset, String metadata)
public OffsetAndMetadata(long offset, int leaderEpoch, String metadata)
```

**Key Concept:** When committing, the offset should be the **next offset to read**, not the last offset processed. If you process offset 4, commit offset 5.

### OffsetCommitCallback

Functional interface for asynchronous commit completion.

```java
@FunctionalInterface
public interface OffsetCommitCallback {
    /**
     * @param offsets Map of offsets that were committed
     * @param exception null if successful, exception otherwise
     * @throws CommitFailedException if commit failed and cannot be retried
     * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.RebalanceInProgressException if rebalance occurred
     */
    void onComplete(Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndMetadata> offsets, Exception exception);
}
```

**Execution Context:** Callbacks may execute in any thread calling `poll()`. However, they are guaranteed to execute before any subsequent `commitSync()` call completes.

### OffsetAndTimestamp

Result of timestamp-based offset lookup via `offsetsForTimes()`.

**Fields:**
- `Long offset`: The offset corresponding to the given timestamp (null if not found)
- `Long timestamp`: The timestamp of the record at the returned offset

---

## Consumer Lifecycle

### Constructor Variants

#### 1. Configuration Map Constructor

```java
public KafkaConsumer(Map<String, Object> configs)
```

- Takes a map of configuration strings to values
- Deserializers must be specified in the config map via `key.deserializer` and `value.deserializer` properties

#### 2. Properties Constructor

```java
public KafkaConsumer(Properties properties)
```

- Takes a `Properties` object with configuration
- Deserializers specified via string-based properties

#### 3. Properties with Explicit Deserializers

```java
public KafkaConsumer(Properties properties,
                     Deserializer<K> keyDeserializer,
                     Deserializer<V> valueDeserializer)
```

- Allows passing deserializer instances directly
- Deserializer `configure()` method will **not** be called when passed directly

#### 4. Config Map with Explicit Deserializers

```java
public KafkaConsumer(Map<String, Object> configs,
                     Deserializer<K> keyDeserializer,
                     Deserializer<V> valueDeserializer)
```

**Configuration Note:** Valid configuration strings are documented in `ConsumerConfig` class. Key configs affecting behavior include:

- `bootstrap.servers`: Initial broker list for cluster discovery
- `group.id`: Consumer group identifier (required for group management)
- `enable.auto.commit`: Whether to automatically commit offsets (default: true)
- `auto.commit.interval.ms`: Interval between automatic commits (default: 5000ms)
- `max.poll.interval.ms`: Maximum time between polls before proactive group exit (default: 300000ms)
- `session.timeout.ms`: Time before broker considers consumer dead (default: 45000ms)
- `heartbeat.interval.ms`: Frequency of group coordinator heartbeats (default: 3000ms)
- `isolation.level`: `read_uncommitted` (default) or `read_committed` for transactional reads

### Close and Cleanup

#### Synchronous Close

```java
public void close()
public void close(Duration timeout)
```

- Closes all connections and releases resources
- Blocks up to the specified timeout (or `default.api.timeout.ms` if not specified)
- Triggers `onPartitionsRevoked()` callbacks via consumer group protocol
- Waits for any pending async commits to complete
- **Must be called** in a finally block or try-with-resources to prevent connection leaks

#### Close with Options

```java
public void close(CloseOptions option)
```

- Supports `leaveGroup()` option for proactive group exit (vs graceful handoff)

**Example:**
```java
try (KafkaConsumer<String, String> consumer = new KafkaConsumer<>(props)) {
    consumer.subscribe(Arrays.asList("topic"));
    while (running) {
        ConsumerRecords<String, String> records = consumer.poll(Duration.ofMillis(100));
        // process records
    }
} // close() called automatically
```

---

## Subscription and Assignment

### Dynamic Subscription (Group Management)

#### Subscribe to Topics with Listener

```java
public void subscribe(Collection<String> topics, ConsumerRebalanceListener listener)
```

**Behavior:**
- Replaces the previous subscription (not incremental)
- Enables consumer group coordination and automatic partition assignment
- Triggers rebalance when:
  - Partition count for subscribed topics changes
  - New topic matching subscription is created
  - Consumer group membership changes
  - Subscribed topic is deleted
- Cannot be mixed with manual partition assignment

**Exceptions:**
- `IllegalArgumentException`: If topics is null, contains nulls, or listener is null
- `IllegalStateException`: If `subscribe(Pattern)` or `assign()` was previously called without `unsubscribe()`

#### Subscribe to Topics (No Listener)

```java
public void subscribe(Collection<String> topics)
```

Equivalent to `subscribe(topics, ConsumerRebalanceListener.noop())`. No rebalance callbacks are invoked.

#### Subscribe to Topic Pattern

```java
public void subscribe(Pattern pattern, ConsumerRebalanceListener listener)
public void subscribe(Pattern pattern)
```

Subscribes to topics matching the regex pattern. New topics matching the pattern are detected through periodic metadata refreshes and trigger rebalances.

**Pattern Validation:** Throws `InvalidRegularExpression` if the pattern is invalid.

#### Subscribe to SubscriptionPattern

```java
public void subscribe(SubscriptionPattern pattern, ConsumerRebalanceListener listener)
public void subscribe(SubscriptionPattern pattern)
```

Advanced pattern type allowing more fine-grained topic matching.

### Manual Assignment (Standalone Mode)

#### Assign Specific Partitions

```java
public void assign(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions)
```

**Behavior:**
- Replaces previous assignment (not incremental)
- If empty, equivalent to `unsubscribe()`
- Disables consumer group coordination and rebalancing
- No rebalance callbacks are triggered
- Each consumer acts independently
- Cannot be mixed with dynamic topic subscription

**Offsets:**
- Initial offsets determined by:
  1. Last committed offset (if available)
  2. Offset set via `seek()`
  3. Reset policy (if no committed offset and no seek)

**Exceptions:**
- `IllegalArgumentException`: If partitions is null or contains nulls/empty topics
- `IllegalStateException`: If `subscribe()` was previously called without `unsubscribe()`

### Query Subscription/Assignment

#### Get Current Assignment

```java
public Set<TopicPartition> assignment()
```

Returns partitions currently assigned to this consumer. Empty if using topic subscription and rebalance hasn't completed yet.

#### Get Current Subscription

```java
public Set<String> subscription()
```

Returns topics subscribed to via `subscribe()`. Empty set if using manual assignment or no subscription.

### Unsubscribe

#### Remove All Subscriptions and Assignments

```java
public void unsubscribe()
```

- Clears both topic subscriptions and manual partition assignments
- Triggers `onPartitionsRevoked()` callback (if using group management)
- Consumer enters an idle state with no data sources

---

## Polling and Data Fetching

### Poll for Records

#### Main Poll Method

```java
public ConsumerRecords<K, V> poll(final Duration timeout)
```

**Behavior:**
- **Returns immediately** if records are available OR position advances past control records (in `read_committed` mode)
- **Blocks up to timeout** if no data available, waiting for:
  - Records to arrive from brokers
  - Fetch request to complete
  - Control records or aborted transactions to be skipped
- **May block longer than timeout** if:
  - Rebalance callbacks are executing (callbacks don't count toward timeout)
  - Group coordination is happening
  - Metadata refresh is in progress
- Returns empty `ConsumerRecords` if timeout expires with no new data

**Critical Behavioral Note on Rebalancing:**
- Rebalances only occur during `poll()` calls
- Callbacks execute as part of `poll()` and may extend total blocking time
- After `poll()` returns, assignment may have changed
- The timeout parameter is for network fetches, **not** for callback execution

**Offset Advancement:**
- `position()` automatically advances as records are fetched
- If fetching from offset 100-104, position becomes 105 after poll returns
- Gaps may occur with compacted topics or filtered transactional messages

**Parameters:**
- `timeout`: Maximum time to wait for data. Must not exceed `Long.MAX_VALUE` milliseconds.

**Return Value:**
- `ConsumerRecords<K, V>`: Map-like collection of records by partition
- Empty collection if timeout expires
- Never null

**Exceptions:**
- `IllegalStateException`: If no topics/partitions are subscribed/assigned
- `InvalidOffsetException`: If offset is undefined/out of range and no reset policy configured
- `WakeupException`: If `wakeup()` was called before/during execution
- `InterruptException`: If thread was interrupted before/during execution
- `AuthenticationException`: If authentication fails
- `AuthorizationException`: If lacking read access to topics or group ID
- `TimeoutException`: If fetch metadata cannot be retrieved within timeout
- `InvalidTopicException`: If subscribed topics are invalid
- `FencedInstanceIdException`: If consumer is fenced by broker (instance ID changed)

**Reset Policy Interaction:**
The `auto.offset.reset` configuration controls behavior when offset is invalid:
- `earliest`: Seek to beginning of partition
- `latest`: Seek to end of partition
- `none`: Throw `InvalidOffsetException`

### Poll Semantics Diagram

```
poll() call
├─ Check for rebalance needed
│  └─ Execute ConsumerRebalanceListener callbacks (extends blocking time)
├─ Fetch data from brokers (up to timeout)
├─ Skip control records (if read_committed)
├─ Deserialize records
└─ Return ConsumerRecords (may be empty)

time_spent = max(rebalance_callback_time, fetch_time)
             (NOT timeout + callback_time, callbacks extend the timeout)
```

### Practical Polling Loop

```java
// Standard polling loop
while (running) {
    try {
        ConsumerRecords<String, String> records = consumer.poll(Duration.ofMillis(100));
        for (ConsumerRecord<String, String> record : records) {
            // Process record
            processRecord(record);
        }
        // Commit after processing
        consumer.commitAsync();
    } catch (WakeupException e) {
        // Expected during shutdown
        break;
    }
}
```

---

## Offset Management

### Core Offset Concepts

#### Position
- The **offset of the next record to be fetched**
- Automatically advanced by `poll()` as records are returned
- Can be manually set via `seek()`
- Returned by `position(TopicPartition)`

**Key Relationship:** If `position()` is N, the next `poll()` will fetch starting from offset N.

#### Committed Offset
- The **offset of the next record to read** after restart
- Stored durably by Kafka broker
- Retrieved by `committed(Set<TopicPartition>)`
- Updated via `commitSync()`, `commitAsync()`, or automatic commits

**Commitment Rule:** When committing, the offset should be the **next offset to read**, not the last offset processed.

```java
// Correct: commit the offset of the next message to read
consumer.commitSync(Collections.singletonMap(
    partition,
    new OffsetAndMetadata(lastProcessedRecord.offset() + 1)
));

// Or use ConsumerRecords.nextOffsets()
consumer.commitSync(records.nextOffsets());
```

#### Beginning and End Offsets
- Beginning offset: The earliest offset available in a partition
- End offset: The offset **after** the last message
- For `read_committed`: End offset is the LSO (Last Stable Offset)

### Automatic Offset Commits

#### Configuration

- `enable.auto.commit`: Set to `true` to enable (default)
- `auto.commit.interval.ms`: Time between commits (default: 5000ms)

#### Behavior

- Commits the current `position()` automatically at interval
- Commits happen asynchronously (not during `poll()` calls)
- If consumer crashes between commits, messages between last commit and crash may be reprocessed
- Safe for "at-least-once" delivery semantics
- All records from `poll()` must be processed before next `poll()` call to avoid committed offset advancing ahead of processed records

#### Rebalance Behavior

- Auto-commits are triggered before rebalance starts
- A final commit happens during `close()`

### Manual Synchronous Commits

#### Commit All Consumed Offsets

```java
public void commitSync()
public void commitSync(Duration timeout)
```

**Behavior:**
- Blocks until commit succeeds or error occurs
- Commits the current `position()` for all assigned partitions
- Uses `default.api.timeout.ms` if no timeout specified
- Retries on transient errors until timeout

**Return:** No return value (void)

#### Commit Specific Offsets

```java
public void commitSync(Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndMetadata> offsets)
public void commitSync(Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndMetadata> offsets, Duration timeout)
```

**Behavior:**
- Commits explicitly specified offsets per partition
- Allows fine-grained control over which offsets are committed
- Offsets must be for currently assigned partitions (if using group management)
- Map is copied internally; safe to mutate after returning

**Use Case:** Commit after processing each partition's records separately
```java
ConsumerRecords<String, String> records = consumer.poll(Duration.ofMillis(100));
for (TopicPartition partition : records.partitions()) {
    List<ConsumerRecord<String, String>> partitionRecords = records.records(partition);
    for (ConsumerRecord<String, String> record : partitionRecords) {
        processRecord(record);
    }
    // Commit after this partition is fully processed
    long nextOffset = records.nextOffsets().get(partition).offset();
    consumer.commitSync(Collections.singletonMap(
        partition,
        new OffsetAndMetadata(nextOffset)
    ));
}
```

**Exceptions:**
- `CommitFailedException`: Commit failed and cannot be retried (fatal in group management)
- `RebalanceInProgressException`: Consumer in middle of rebalance
- `WakeupException`: `wakeup()` called
- `InterruptException`: Thread interrupted
- `TimeoutException`: Timeout expired before commit

### Manual Asynchronous Commits

#### Commit All Consumed Offsets (Async)

```java
public void commitAsync()
public void commitAsync(OffsetCommitCallback callback)
```

**Behavior:**
- Returns immediately without blocking
- Commits the current `position()` for all assigned partitions
- Callback invoked when commit completes (or immediately if null)
- Multiple async commits are ordered; callbacks invoked in order
- Async commits complete before subsequent `commitSync()` calls return

#### Commit Specific Offsets (Async)

```java
public void commitAsync(Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndMetadata> offsets,
                        OffsetCommitCallback callback)
```

**Behavior:**
- Commits explicitly specified offsets asynchronously
- Callback receives the committed offsets and any exception

**Callback Example:**
```java
consumer.commitAsync(new OffsetCommitCallback() {
    public void onComplete(Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndMetadata> offsets, Exception exception) {
        if (exception != null) {
            log.error("Commit failed for offsets: " + offsets, exception);
        } else {
            log.info("Commit succeeded for offsets: " + offsets);
        }
    }
});
```

**Callback Exceptions:**
- `CommitFailedException`: Cannot be retried
- `RebalanceInProgressException`: Can retry after rebalance
- `WakeupException`: Can rethrow or ignore
- `InterruptException`: Can rethrow or ignore

### Offset Reset Policies

#### Configuration

- `auto.offset.reset`: How to initialize offset if not found

#### Behavior

Applied when:
- No committed offset exists for partition
- Offset is out of range (log deleted, partition recreated)
- Explicit offset not set via `seek()`

#### Options

- `earliest`: Start from beginning offset (offset 0 or first available)
- `latest`: Start from end offset (next offset after last message)
- `none`: Throw `InvalidOffsetException` (fail fast, explicit offset required)

### Query Committed Offsets

#### Get Committed Offsets

```java
public Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndMetadata> committed(Set<TopicPartition> partitions)
public Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndMetadata> committed(Set<TopicPartition> partitions,
                                                        Duration timeout)
```

**Behavior:**
- Returns committed offsets for specified partitions
- Makes remote call to broker to fetch latest committed values
- Returns null for partitions with no committed offset
- Map key is `TopicPartition`, value is `OffsetAndMetadata` or null
- Blocks until commit status retrieved or timeout expires

**Parameters:**
- `partitions`: Which partitions to query
- `timeout`: Maximum wait time (uses `default.api.timeout.ms` if not specified)

**Return:** Map with entries only for partitions with committed offsets

---

## Flow Control and Position Management

### Pause and Resume

#### Pause Consuming from Partitions

```java
public void pause(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions)
```

**Behavior:**
- Pauses fetching from specified partitions
- Records already fetched remain available via `poll()`
- `poll()` will not fetch new records from paused partitions
- Already-fetched records must be consumed before resuming

**Use Cases:**
- Back-pressure handling: Pause faster partitions while slower ones catch up
- Stream processing: Pause input while processing previous batch
- Rebalancing: Pause to prevent new data during critical sections

#### Resume Consuming from Partitions

```java
public void resume(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions)
```

**Behavior:**
- Resumes fetching from previously paused partitions
- No-op if partition was not paused
- Next `poll()` will fetch new records from resumed partitions

#### Query Paused Partitions

```java
public Set<TopicPartition> paused()
```

**Return:** Set of currently paused partitions

### Position Control

#### Seek to Specific Offset

```java
public void seek(TopicPartition partition, long offset)
public void seek(TopicPartition partition, OffsetAndMetadata offsetAndMetadata)
```

**Behavior:**
- Sets the `position()` to the specified offset
- Effective on next `poll()` call
- Multiple seeks for same partition replace previous seeks
- Only affects fetching, not committed offset

**Variant with Metadata:**
- Second form includes leader epoch information
- Preserves epoch across consumer restarts for data consistency
- Epoch obtained from `ConsumerRecord#leaderEpoch()` or `ConsumerRecords#nextOffsets()`

**Exceptions:**
- `IllegalArgumentException`: If offset is negative
- `IllegalStateException`: If partition not assigned

**Use Case:** Restart from custom offset store
```java
consumer.assign(Arrays.asList(new TopicPartition("topic", 0)));
long savedOffset = externalOffsetStore.getOffset("topic", 0);
consumer.seek(new TopicPartition("topic", 0), savedOffset);
```

#### Seek to Beginning

```java
public void seekToBeginning(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions)
```

**Behavior:**
- Sets position to the earliest offset in each partition
- Lazy evaluation: actual seek happens during `poll()` or `position()` call
- Empty partition list seeks all assigned partitions
- Allows re-consuming all available data

#### Seek to End

```java
public void seekToEnd(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions)
```

**Behavior:**
- Sets position to the end offset (first offset after last message)
- Lazy evaluation: actual seek happens during `poll()` or `position()` call
- Empty partition list seeks all assigned partitions
- Useful for skipping backlog and consuming only new data

**Transactional Behavior:**
- With `isolation.level=read_committed`: Seeks to LSO (Last Stable Offset), not absolute end
- LSO is the offset of the first message in an open transaction
- Allows reading only committed transactional messages

#### Get Current Position

```java
public long position(TopicPartition partition)
public long position(TopicPartition partition, final Duration timeout)
```

**Behavior:**
- Returns the offset of the next record that will be fetched
- May issue remote call if position not yet determined
- Blocks until position determined or timeout expires
- Triggers lazy seeks set via `seekToBeginning()` or `seekToEnd()`

**Return:** The current position (next offset to fetch)

**Exceptions:**
- `IllegalStateException`: If partition not assigned
- `InvalidOffsetException`: If position undefined (no committed offset, no seek, out of range)
- `TimeoutException`: If position cannot be determined before timeout

### Timestamp-Based Offset Lookup

#### Search for Offsets by Timestamp

```java
public Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndTimestamp> offsetsForTimes(Map<TopicPartition, Long> timestampsToSearch)
public Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndTimestamp> offsetsForTimes(Map<TopicPartition, Long> timestampsToSearch,
                                                               Duration timeout)
```

**Behavior:**
- Finds the offset for each partition's first message with timestamp >= given timestamp
- Makes remote call to brokers
- Returns null if timestamp not found (no message with that timestamp exists)
- Blocks until offsets retrieved or timeout expires

**Parameters:**
- `timestampsToSearch`: Map of partition to timestamp (milliseconds since epoch)
- `timeout`: Maximum wait time

**Return:** Map of partition to `OffsetAndTimestamp` (null if not found)

**Use Case:** Seek to messages from specific time
```java
Map<TopicPartition, Long> timestampsToSearch = new HashMap<>();
timestampsToSearch.put(partition, System.currentTimeMillis() - 3600000); // 1 hour ago

Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndTimestamp> offsets = consumer.offsetsForTimes(timestampsToSearch);
for (Map.Entry<TopicPartition, OffsetAndTimestamp> entry : offsets.entrySet()) {
    if (entry.getValue() != null) {
        consumer.seek(entry.getKey(), entry.getValue().offset());
    }
}
```

#### Get Beginning Offsets

```java
public Map<TopicPartition, Long> beginningOffsets(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions)
public Map<TopicPartition, Long> beginningOffsets(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions,
                                                  Duration timeout)
```

**Return:** Map of partition to earliest available offset

**Note:** Does not seek; only returns the offset value.

#### Get End Offsets

```java
public Map<TopicPartition, Long> endOffsets(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions)
public Map<TopicPartition, Long> endOffsets(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions,
                                           Duration timeout)
```

**Return:** Map of partition to end offset (first offset after last message)

**Transactional Note:** With `isolation.level=read_committed`, returns LSO instead of absolute end.

---

## Rebalancing and Group Coordination

### Consumer Groups Overview

A consumer group is a set of consumer instances sharing the same `group.id` that collectively consume a topic's partitions. Kafka automatically assigns partitions to group members.

**Key Properties:**
- Only one consumer group member receives each partition
- Partitions distribute across group members
- Instances can dynamically join/leave
- Broker manages group coordination and partition rebalancing

### Group Membership and Lifecycle

#### Initial Join

- Consumer joins group on first `poll()` call after `subscribe()`
- Triggers a rebalance to redistribute partitions
- Assignment delayed until rebalance completes

#### Session Management

**session.timeout.ms:** Time before broker considers consumer dead (default: 45000ms)
- If no heartbeat received for this duration, consumer removed from group
- Triggers rebalance with other members

**heartbeat.interval.ms:** Frequency of group heartbeats (default: 3000ms)
- Recommended: `session.timeout.ms / 3`
- Sent periodically; failure to send triggers timeout

**max.poll.interval.ms:** Maximum time between polls (default: 300000ms)
- If consumer doesn't call `poll()` within this interval, proactively exits group
- Prevents "livelock" where consumer sends heartbeats but makes no progress
- Exceeding this causes `CommitFailedException` on commit attempts

#### Proactive Group Exit

When consumer exceeds `max.poll.interval.ms`:
1. Consumer proactively leaves the group
2. `onPartitionsRevoked()` triggered on next `poll()` call
3. Partitions reassigned to other group members
4. Subsequent commits fail with `CommitFailedException`

This is a **safety mechanism** ensuring only active members commit offsets.

### Rebalance Triggering Events

Rebalances occur when:
- New consumer joins group (detected during heartbeat)
- Consumer leaves group (gracefully or timeout)
- Partition count changes for subscribed topics
- Subscribed topic created/deleted
- New topic matching subscribed pattern created
- Consumer fails to send heartbeat
- `enforceRebalance()` called

### Rebalance Protocols

#### Classic (Eager) Protocol
- **Name:** `GroupProtocol.CLASSIC`
- `onPartitionsRevoked()` called with **full assignment** (all partitions revoked)
- All members must participate before assignment completes
- More disruptive, all members must rebalance simultaneously

#### Cooperative (Incremental) Protocol
- **Name:** `GroupProtocol.CONSUMER` or cooperative assignments
- `onPartitionsRevoked()` called only with **revoked partitions** (if non-empty)
- Members can continue consuming stable partitions during rebalance
- Less disruptive, only revoked partitions stop temporarily

**Configuration:** `group.protocol` (legacy: `partition.assignment.strategy`)

### Rebalance Listener Callbacks

#### Callback Execution Order

Under normal conditions:
1. **onPartitionsRevoked()** - Revoked partitions leaving this consumer
2. *Rebalance at broker*
3. **onPartitionsAssigned()** - New partitions assigned to this consumer

#### onPartitionsRevoked Semantics

```java
public void onPartitionsRevoked(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions) {
    // Save offsets, flush caches, release resources
    // Called with full assignment under eager rebalance
    // Called with subset under cooperative rebalance
}
```

**Recommended Actions:**
- Commit offsets to Kafka or external store
- Flush any accumulated state
- Close external connections tied to partitions
- Clear partition-specific caches

#### onPartitionsAssigned Semantics

```java
public void onPartitionsAssigned(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions) {
    // Initialize positions, load external state, warm caches
    // Called even if partitions is empty (signals rebalance completion)
}
```

**Recommended Actions:**
- Initialize position for new partitions
- Load state from external offset store
- Warm up partition-specific caches
- Do NOT start consuming yet (happens automatically after callback returns)

**Guaranteed:** All revocations complete before any member's `onPartitionsAssigned()` executes.

#### onPartitionsLost Semantics

```java
public void onPartitionsLost(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions) {
    // Handle unexpected loss, don't commit offsets
    // Partitions may already belong to other members
}
```

**When Invoked:**
- Session timeout occurred
- Fatal error received from broker
- Not a graceful revocation

**Action:** Do not attempt to commit offsets; partitions may already belong to others.

### Enforce Rebalance

#### Trigger Rebalance Manually

```java
public void enforceRebalance()
public void enforceRebalance(final String reason)
```

**Behavior:**
- Signals coordinator that rebalance should occur
- Rebalance happens on next `poll()` call
- Useful for updating group composition without timeout

**Use Case:** Remove consumer from group on-demand without waiting for timeout
```java
consumer.enforceRebalance();
try {
    consumer.poll(Duration.ofSeconds(1)); // Wait for rebalance
} catch (InterruptException e) {
    // May be interrupted during rebalance
}
```

### Group Metadata

#### Get Group Information

```java
public ConsumerGroupMetadata groupMetadata()
```

**Return:** Metadata about current group membership including:
- Group ID
- Generation ID
- Member ID
- Partition assignment strategy

**Use Case:** Pass to transactional producers for exactly-once semantics
```java
ConsumerGroupMetadata metadata = consumer.groupMetadata();
// Use in producer transactions to coordinate exactly-once processing
```

---

## Transactional Message Consumption

### Isolation Levels

The `isolation.level` configuration controls how transactional messages are read.

#### read_uncommitted (Default)

- Reads all messages including uncommitted transactional messages
- No client-side buffering
- Most throughput
- Messages may roll back if transaction aborts

#### read_committed

- Reads only committed transactional messages
- Skips aborted transaction messages
- LSO (Last Stable Offset) boundaries
- Better consistency guarantees

### Reading in read_committed Mode

#### Message Filtering

- Consumer automatically filters out aborted transaction markers
- Transaction commit/abort markers not returned to application (but consume offsets)
- Gap in offsets indicates filtered messages

**Example:**
```
Offsets: 1, 2, 3, [4-6 aborted and filtered], 7, 8, 9
Returned: 1, 2, 3, 7, 8, 9
```

#### Last Stable Offset (LSO)

The LSO is the offset of the first message in an open transaction. Consumer blocks at LSO until:
- Transaction commits (LSO advances)
- Transaction aborts (transaction filtered, LSO advances)
- Timeout occurs

**Effect on Offset Queries:**
- `position()` does not advance past LSO
- `seekToEnd()` seeks to LSO, not absolute end
- `endOffsets()` returns LSO, not absolute end

#### No Client-Side Buffering

Unlike `read_uncommitted`:
- Messages are not buffered waiting for transaction commit
- Consumer simply stops at LSO if transaction still open
- Next `poll()` returns empty until LSO advances

### Consuming Exactly-Once with Transactions

```java
// Consumer only reads committed messages
Properties props = new Properties();
props.setProperty("isolation.level", "read_committed");

// Producer writes transactions
// Consumer reads committed messages atomically
// Coordinate via ConsumerGroupMetadata for exactly-once processing
```

---

## Metadata and Monitoring

### Partition Information

#### Get Partition Metadata

```java
public List<PartitionInfo> partitionsFor(String topic)
public List<PartitionInfo> partitionsFor(String topic, Duration timeout)
```

**Behavior:**
- Returns metadata about all partitions for a topic
- Makes remote call if metadata not cached
- Returns empty list if topic doesn't exist

**Return Value:** List of `PartitionInfo` objects containing:
- Partition ID
- Leader broker ID
- Replica broker IDs
- In-sync replica (ISR) broker IDs

#### List All Topics

```java
public Map<String, List<PartitionInfo>> listTopics()
public Map<String, List<PartitionInfo>> listTopics(Duration timeout)
```

**Behavior:**
- Returns metadata for all topics in cluster
- May issue remote call
- Blocks until metadata retrieved or timeout expires

**Return:** Map from topic name to partition list

### Metrics

#### Get Consumer Metrics

```java
public Map<MetricName, ? extends Metric> metrics()
```

**Behavior:**
- Returns all metrics tracked by consumer
- Includes:
  - Fetch rates and latencies
  - Commit success/failure rates
  - Consumer lag per partition
  - Rebalance metrics
  - Network metrics

**Use Case:** Custom monitoring and alerting
```java
Map<MetricName, ? extends Metric> metrics = consumer.metrics();
for (Map.Entry<MetricName, ? extends Metric> entry : metrics.entrySet()) {
    MetricName name = entry.getKey();
    double value = entry.getValue().value();
    // Report metric
}
```

### Consumer Lag

#### Get Current Lag

```java
public OptionalLong currentLag(TopicPartition topicPartition)
```

**Behavior:**
- Returns the difference between end offset and current position
- `OptionalLong.empty()` if lag cannot be determined
- Non-blocking call

**Semantics:**
```
lag = endOffset - position()

// If consumer at position 100, end offset 150:
// lag = 150 - 100 = 50 messages behind
```

**Use Case:** Detect slow consumers
```java
OptionalLong lag = consumer.currentLag(partition);
if (lag.isPresent() && lag.getAsLong() > 10000) {
    log.warn("Consumer lagging by " + lag.getAsLong() + " messages");
}
```

### Client Telemetry

#### Get Client Instance ID

```java
public Uuid clientInstanceId(Duration timeout)
```

**Behavior:**
- Returns unique ID for this consumer instance
- Used for correlating telemetry
- Requires telemetry enabled: `enable.metrics.push=true`
- First call generates ID via remote request
- Blocks until ID obtained or timeout expires

**Exception:**
- `IllegalStateException`: If telemetry not enabled
